- **What happens if you skip it:** Companies cannot use their OPCO budget to fund your training; you lose 40-60% of potential enterprise clients in continuous training market
- **Ongoing:** Each OPCO may audit; OPCO may request data on trainee satisfaction, completion, outcomes

### **Authority 7 — France Compétences**
- **Status:** CONDITIONAL (for any RNCP/RS-registered certification you offer)
- **Legal basis:** Loi Avenir Professionnel 2018; Code du travail Art. L.6113-3 et seq.
- **What:** Registration check + oversight of habilitation (is your SIRET listed as partner of the certificating body?)
- **Your case:** CELTA is RS #3262 (owner: Cambridge Assessment English). France Compétences holds the official registry.
- **Process:** 
  - Automatic: Once Cambridge issues habilitation, they declare you to France Compétences
  - France Compétences publishes public list of habilitated partners
  - You appear on France Compétences platform as authorized CELTA provider
- **Oversight:** France Compétences monitors data submitted by Cambridge on your center's pass rates, candidate demographics, outcomes. Can request removal if data shows serious issues.
- **Duration:** Continuous while habilitation valid
- **What happens if you skip it:** If Cambridge does not declare you or removes you from their list, France Compétences removes you; CPF eligibility disappears
- **Ongoing:** Transparency obligation — France Compétences publishes pass rate data

---

## PHASE 4 — ONGOING OPERATIONAL AUTHORIZATIONS & INSPECTIONS

### **Authority 8 — DREETS SRC (Service Régional de Contrôle — Regional Control Service)**
- **Status:** MANDATORY (ongoing inspection authority)
- **Legal basis:** Article L.6354-1 et seq. Code du travail
- **Frequency:** No fixed schedule; risk-based (high-risk sectors, fraud indicators, complaints)
- **Scope:** Administrative and financial controls (not pedagogical quality, which is Qualiopi's domain)
  - Verification of BPF accuracy
  - Financial compliance (use of funds)
  - Adherence to training contract obligations
  - Disability accommodation procedures (Art. L.5213-6)
- **Duration of control:** 2–9 months cumulative over 3 years (capped under Charte de contrôle)
- **What happens if violations found:** 
  - Warning + remediation order
  - Fine (up to €6,000 per violation)
  - Suspension of NDA (temporary)
  - Revocation of NDA (permanent) — Art. R6351-6
- **Rights:** You get a final report; can appeal to tribunal administratif

### **Authority 9 — Qualiopi Auditor (ongoing surveillance)**
- **Status:** MANDATORY (post-certification)
- **Legal basis:** Qualiopi specification (CNEFOP/COFRAC standard)
- **Frequency:** Auditors conduct random surveillance audits; at minimum once during 3-year validity period
- **Scope:** All 6 indicators / 32 criteria including Indicateur 26 (disability accessibility)
- **Duration:** 1–2 days typical
- **What happens if non-compliance found:** 
  - Minor non-conformities: corrective action plan (CAP) — must fix within 2 months
  - Major non-conformities: certificate suspension immediately; final audit within 3 months to confirm remediation
  - If not remediated: Certificate revoked (automatic removal from DREETS public list, CDC/EDOF, OPCO platforms)

### **Authority 10 — Cambridge Assessment English (ongoing centre monitoring)**
- **Status:** MANDATORY (for CELTA certification)
- **Legal basis:** Cambridge Centre Handbook; partnership agreement between centre and Cambridge
- **What:** Cambridge performs regular centre compliance audits (distinct from pedagogical assessment)
  - Administrative procedures (health & safety, accessibility, candidate welfare)
  - Examiner training & credential verification
  - Record-keeping (portfolios, assessments, appeals processes)
  - Equal opportunities policy implementation ← **ELT violated this**
- **Frequency:** Minimum once during accreditation period (typically 3–4 years); risk-based otherwise
- **Scope includes:** Verification that you have:
  - Documented equal opportunities policy
  - Disability contact person (ELT: Jane Ryder — **but never activated**)
  - Accessible facilities
  - Appeals procedure (accessible & responsive)
- **Duration:** Typically 1–2 days
- **Outcomes:** 
  - Full accreditation (continue)
  - Accreditation with conditions (fix issues within 3 months)
  - Probation (risks identified; close monitoring)
  - Accreditation suspension (serious breaches)
  - Deaccreditation (permanent loss of right to offer CELTA)
- **What happens if deaccreditation:** You cannot legally award CELTA certificates; existing candidates' results invalidated; candidates sue for damages

---

## CONDITIONAL AUTHORITIES (If applicable)

### **Authority 11 — URSSAF (Union de Recouvrement des Cotisations de Sécurité Sociale)**
- **Status:** CONDITIONAL (if you have employees)
- **What:** Employer social contribution compliance; also conducts audits on training providers to detect undeclared labor (trainers paid as employees vs. self-employed)
- **Frequency:** Random or risk-based
- **Scope:** Payroll, classification (employee vs. self-employed), social contributions, payroll taxes
- **Duration:** Typically 1–3 days on-site
- **What happens if violations found:** 
  - Back-payments (contributions + penalties: 10–40% of underpaid amounts)
  - Fines up to €15,000 per undeclared employee month
- **Rights:** Appeal to URSSAF supervisor; then tribunal

### **Authority 12 — Conseil Régional (Regional Council) — Subvention monitoring**
- **Status:** CONDITIONAL (if you received regional grants or apprenticeship funding)
- **What:** Audit of how regional subsidies were spent; verification of apprenticeship contract compliance
- **Frequency:** Post-subsidy audit, typically 6–12 months after funding cycle closes
- **Scope:** Financial tracking, candidate demographic verification, outcomes reporting
- **Duration:** 2–5 days
- **What happens if violations found:** Demand repayment of improper grants; future grants denied

### **Authority 13 — FSE / FESI / FEM Auditors (European fund monitors)**
- **Status:** CONDITIONAL (if you received or will receive European Social Fund, European Integration Initiative, or Globalization Adjustment Fund)
- **What:** Compliance audit for EU co-financed training (rare in private providers; more common in nonprofit / public contracts)
- **Frequency:** Based on grant schedule
- **Duration:** 2–10 days (extensive documentation review)
- **What happens if violations found:** Fund clawback (repayment of 10–100% of grant depending on severity); ban on future EU funding

---

## SUMMARY TABLE

| Authority | Domain | Mandatory? | Timing | Can revoke? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **INSEE/GFE** | Business registration | YES | Pre-operation | No (but can deactivate SIREN) | One-time only |
| **Préfet de Région/DREETS** | Activity declaration (NDA) | YES | Within 3 months of first contract | YES — Art. R6351-6 | Annual BPF obligation |
| **Cambridge Assessment** | CELTA habilitation | YES (if offering CELTA) | Before public marketing | YES — remove from partners | Annual reporting |
| **Qualiopi Auditor (COFRAC)** | Quality certification | YES (if using CPF/public funds from 2022+) | Before accessing funded training | YES — 3-year validity | Can suspend mid-term |
| **Caisse des Dépôts (CDC)** | CPF marketplace listing | YES (if CPF targeted) | After Qualiopi + Cambridge | YES — auto-remove if Qualiopi lapses | EDOF platform access |
| **OPCO** | Employee training co-financing | CONDITIONAL | Ongoing (no formal approval; automatic via SIREN) | YES — if Qualiopi lapses | 11 parallel operators |
| **France Compétences** | RS/RNCP oversight | CONDITIONAL (for certifications) | Continuous | NO directly (but Cambridge can) | Transparency/publication role |
| **DREETS SRC** | Administrative & financial inspection | YES (ongoing) | Risk-based; capped 2–9 months/3 years | YES — suspend/revoke NDA | Disability compliance (Art. L.5213-6) ← **ELT breached** |
| **Qualiopi surveillance** | Post-certification audit | YES | During 3-year validity | YES — revoke certificate | Random + risk-based |
| **Cambridge ongoing monitoring** | Centre compliance (accessibility, procedures) | YES (if CELTA offered) | Risk-based; min. once/3–4 years | YES — deaccredit centre | Equal opportunities policy ← **ELT breached** |
| **URSSAF** | Employment compliance | YES (if employees) | Risk-based | N/A (can fine + demand back-pay) | Detects undeclared labor |
| **Conseil Régional** | Grant compliance | CONDITIONAL | Post-subsidy | Can demand repayment | Only if regional grants received |
| **FSE/FESI/FEM auditors** | EU fund compliance | CONDITIONAL | Post-grant | Can demand repayment | Only if EU funds received |

---

## KEY ENFORCEMENT TOOLS

| Sanction | Authority | When applied | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| NDA revocation | DREETS SRC | Serious/repeated violations of L.5213-6, disability law, quality | **FATAL** — cannot operate training |
| Qualiopi suspension | COFRAC auditor | Major non-conformities | Temporary (2–3 months typically) |
| Qualiopi revocation | COFRAC auditor | Non-remediation of suspension | **FATAL** — blocks CPF/OPCO/public funding |
| Cambridge deaccreditation | Cambridge Assessment | Equal opportunities breach, accessibility failure, appeals procedures violated | **FATAL** — cannot offer CELTA |
| EDOF removal | CDC | Qualiopi lapses or inactive BPF | **FATAL** — CPF candidates cannot find you |
| Back-pay demand | URSSAF | Undeclared labor detected | Up to 40% penalties on contributions |
| Financial fine | DREETS | Various Code du travail violations | €1,000–€6,000 per violation |
| Grant clawback | Conseil Régional / EU | Improper use of subsidy | 10–100% of grant amount |

---

## CRITICAL POINTS FOR ELT

### What ELT failed to do:

1. **DREETS (Authority 2):** After 25 Jan 2026 health disclosure, **did not activate disability support procedures** required by Art. L.5213-6 Code du travail (inspectable by DREETS SRC)

2. **Cambridge (Authority 3):** **Did not implement equal opportunities policy** named in their centre handbook; Jane Ryder designated as disability contact but never engaged (inspectable by Cambridge ongoing monitoring; **violation of centre accreditation terms**)

3. **Qualiopi (Authority 4):** **Indicateur 26 non-compliance** — "Le prestataire met en œuvre les moyens nécessaires à l'accueil et à l'accompagnement des personnes en situation de handicap" — four aménagements available, zero proposed (detectable in Qualiopi surveillance audit)

### What this means:

- ELT is **simultaneously vulnerable to enforcement action from at least 3 authorities**: DREETS (NDA revocation), Cambridge (deaccreditation), Qualiopi auditor (certificate revocation)
- Any **one of these** being triggered would be **fatal to business**
- **Combined effect:** Loss of all revenue streams (CPF, OPCO, Cambridge certification legitimacy)

---

## BOTTOM LINE

**The answer to "How many authorities must ELT obtain permission from?"**

| Phase | Count | Authorities |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-operation** | 2 mandatory | INSEE/GFE, DREETS |
| **Quality/Certification** | 2 mandatory | Cambridge, Qualiopi auditor |
| **Funding access** | 2–3 conditional | CDC (CPF), OPCO (employees), France Compétences (registration) |
| **Ongoing operations** | 4 mandatory | DREETS SRC, Qualiopi surveillance, Cambridge monitoring, URSSAF (if employees) |
| **Optional funding** | 2 conditional | Conseil Régional, EU fund monitors |
| **TOTAL** | **5 mandatory + 8 conditional** | **≤13 authorities depending on scope** |

**For a typical setup like ELT (private, CPF+OPCO-eligible, CELTA-focused, ~5–10 employees):**
- **Minimum 7 authorities must grant permission/certification**
- **Maximum 10+ if pursuing subsidies**

**Most dangerous for ELT:**
1. DREETS SRC (revoke NDA — kills everything)
2. Cambridge (deaccredit — invalidates certifications)
3. Qualiopi auditor (revoke — blocks all public funding)

---

*Research completed 22 May 2026. Sources: Code du travail (2024), Qualiopi COFRAC standards, France Compétences website, DREETS.gouv.fr, Cambridge Centre Handbook, Loi Avenir Professionnel 2018, Décret 2015-790.*
-e 
---
# FILE: GUIDELINE_for_writers_investigators.md
## Source: /mnt/project/GUIDELINE_for_writers_investigators.md
---

# AI Guideline for Reporters, Writers, and Investigators
## How to find, read, verify, structure, and break down a story — methods, tools, formats, logics, time, and the things that go wrong

**Derived from:** the two CELTA / Cambridge / The ELT Hub working sessions on 15 May 2026, plus the agent's own operational practice during them.
**Audience:** any writer whose work depends on finding, reading, and rendering nuanced material — beat reporters, investigative journalists, longform writers, policy analysts, academic case-writers, documentarians, regulatory writers, and AI agents serving any of the above.

---

## PART 1 — THE FIVE-PHASE SHAPE OF THE WORK

Almost every serious piece of investigative or analytical writing moves through five phases. They are not strictly sequential — phases recur — but they are useful as a checklist.

**Phase 1 — Find.** Locate the documents, sources, and public records that *could* matter. The error here is over-collection (drowning in material) or under-collection (missing the document that would have decided the piece). Both are failures of triage.

**Phase 2 — Read.** Read forensically, not for the argument the document is trying to make. The shift from reading-for-argument to reading-for-admission is the single most important professional habit. Most writers never make it.

**Phase 3 — Verify.** Every claim worth publishing crosses a verification threshold. The threshold rises with the damage the claim does to its subject. A claim that ruins someone's reputation needs more than a claim that names a building.

**Phase 4 — Structure.** Decide what shape the piece takes — news brief, feature, longform, sidebar, op-ed, Q&A, explainer. The structure is not aesthetic; it determines what evidence the piece can carry.

**Phase 5 — Write.** Render in a register the audience and the legal exposure permit. Administrative-neutral for regulatory writing. Narrative-controlled for feature work. Voice-forward for op-ed.

The CELTA dossier ran through all five repeatedly. The agent rebuilt context in Phase 1, did forensic reading in Phase 2, ran live web verification in Phase 3, built the eighteen-authority cartography in Phase 4, and drafted both the Jane Ryder communication and the academic write-up in Phase 5.

---

## PART 2 — METHODS, IN SMALL PARAGRAPHS

### 2.1 Read for admissions, not arguments

The opposing party's own correspondence is more useful than your own argument will ever be. Look for sentences that *explain* (these admit elements they were trying to defend), exhaustive lists (a list of options available is also a list of options not taken), and phrases assuming prior shared knowledge ("as you know," "as we discussed"). Jane Ryder's 1 March email defended the centre by listing four available accommodations — and in defending, admitted that none were offered. Source: legal-evidentiary tradition; transfers cleanly to journalism.

### 2.2 Timestamp to the minute when the document permits

A duty, a deadline, or a knowledge-trigger that runs on a clock should be cited as precisely as the source allows. "16:12" is more powerful than "the same afternoon." For news work this matters most in: regulatory failures, security incidents, financial trades, communications that contradict later denials. Always grab the original metadata. Screenshots without timestamps are weaker evidence than text with them.

### 2.3 The cross-reference matrix

Three columns: what the subject says, what the document shows, what the relevant standard (law, policy, claim) requires. Divergences are the story. This was how the CELTA case revealed its central asymmetry — the centre demanded perfect contemporaneous evidence from the candidate while accepting its own unannotated record amendment. The matrix is your single most useful pre-writing artefact. Build it before drafting any narrative.

### 2.4 Document tiering

Sort every source into four tiers before writing:

- **T1 — primary, in your possession.** Original documents, direct observation, on-record interviews with your subject.
- **T2 — adversarial / subject's own statements.** The subject's emails, public filings, prior interviews, social media. Most valuable for admissions.
- **T3 — contextual.** Background experts, policies, frameworks, prior reporting, scholarly literature.
- **T4 — reference.** Statutes, codes, datasets, dictionaries, glossaries.

Lead with T1, anchor in T2, support with T3, cite T4. Pieces that mix tiers without hierarchy read as muddled because they are.

### 2.5 The cliquet / ratchet logic for parallel work

When multiple bodies can rule on the same facts (regulators, courts, professional associations, certifiers), findings cascade. One ruling becomes evidence in the next. For investigative work this matters because the question is not "will any one of them act?" but "what is the cumulative probability of at least N findings within M months?" In the CELTA case: eighteen authorities, twelve binding; the threshold for a credible outcome was two findings, not eighteen. The same logic applies to corruption investigations, regulatory series, and any beat where parallel proceedings exist.

### 2.6 Verify at the moment of statement

Training data and prior knowledge are *hypotheses to check*, not facts to cite. Authority email addresses, current personnel, fees, article numbers, postal addresses, form availability — all change. In the CELTA work, an authoritative-looking email (`dreets-bretagne@dreets.gouv.fr`) bounced; the correct one (`bretag.controle-fp@dreets.gouv.fr`) only came from live search. Treat every verifiable specific as a search candidate before you put it in the text.

### 2.7 Triangulation: independent confirmation

A single source is a tip. Two independent sources is a working claim. Three is publishable in most outlets. "Independent" means not derived from the same upstream — two reporters citing the same press release are one source, not two. For documents: a document plus a corroborating witness plus a public record is the standard for serious allegations.

### 2.8 Recognise PR and spin language

Phrases that should trigger a re-read: "robust process," "we take this seriously," "in line with policy," "appropriate steps were taken," "an independent review found." Each is a signal that the document is doing PR work, not informational work. They are not lies, but they are not information. The information is what the document *does not say* in those phrases.

### 2.9 Read for what is *missing*

Absence is evidence when the document's own structure shows what should be there. A tutorial template with a blank "other issues" field two days after a disability disclosure says something. An assessor report covering TP4 and TP7 but not TP8 says something. A press release that names every executive except one says something. The missing entry method works any time you have a template, a list, or a comprehensive scope.

### 2.10 Administrative neutrality as a register

For regulatory submissions, complaint letters, and most investigative writing meant to survive legal review: drop emotional language. State facts. Cite articles by number. Concede legitimate scope. State consequences as facts ("if no substantive response within thirty days, this will be filed with X"). Threats weaken; stated next-steps strengthen. The CELTA Jane Ryder letter was deliberately silent on her conduct — her silence was allowed to speak for itself in the record.

### 2.11 Lead with the admission, not the grievance

Audiences engage differently with "the centre admitted in writing that four accommodations existed and none were offered" than with "I was treated unfairly." The first is a fact about the document; the second is a feeling about an event. Both may be true; only the first compels. This applies to features, op-eds, briefs, court filings, and pitches to editors.

### 2.12 Length discipline

Authorities, editors, and most readers do not finish long pieces. Target your decisive facts to survive every cut. The narrative is what you trim. Email body 300–500 words; feature 1,200–2,500; longform 4,000–8,000; investigative report 6,000–12,000. Going longer requires either subject-matter density that genuinely demands it or a narrative arc that earns the reader's time minute by minute.

### 2.13 Externalise method to a portable file

Whenever a working pattern stabilises across a case, write it down as a standalone skill/memo file. The CELTA work produced three such files. The logic: agents (and human collaborators) reset across sessions; files don't. For an investigator, this is the "case file methodology" doc that survives staff turnover. For an AI agent it is literally a SKILL.md.

### 2.14 Restraint when the case is structurally complete

There is a point at which adding more material weakens the dossier. Once the central admission is in writing and the documentary core is locked, further pushing dilutes the position. Recognise this point. In the CELTA case it came when Jane Ryder confirmed the UK data transfer — after that, the agent told the user to *stop sending material*. For reporters: there is a point past which more reporting does not improve the story and starts to risk it. Editors call this "killing it with kindness."

### 2.15 Recognise stalling tactics

When a subject responds with "I don't understand what you're asking, please simplify" — this is rarely sincere confusion. It is delay. The professional response: re-state the demand in three to five specific items, tie each to a piece of evidence, state the consequence of non-response, end the exchange. The clock continues to run regardless of whether the subject "understands."

---

## PART 3 — TOOLS, BY CATEGORY

A non-exhaustive map of what the work actually requires. AI agents should know which to call; human writers should know which to subscribe to or learn.

### 3.1 Search and retrieval

Web search (current, not training-data); Google Scholar for academic literature; Archive.org / Wayback Machine for vanished pages; the Internet Archive's TV News Archive for broadcast claims; reverse image search (TinEye, Google Images, Yandex) for image provenance; specialised legal databases (Legifrance for France, BAILII for UK, CourtListener for US).

### 3.2 Public records and corporate registries

France: SIRENE / Infogreffe for company data; Légifrance for statute; CADA process for administrative documents; HATVP for officials' declarations. UK: Companies House; Charity Commission; Land Registry; FOI requests via WhatDoTheyKnow. US: PACER for federal courts; SEC EDGAR for filings; state SOS for company registration; MuckRock for FOIA. EU: OpenCorporates aggregates many; Transparency Register for lobbying.

### 3.3 Document analysis

PDF text extraction (`pdftotext`, `extract-text`); OCR for scans (Tesseract, ABBYY); metadata extraction (`exiftool` for images, `pdfinfo` for PDFs); structured-data extraction from tables; diff tools for comparing document versions; the document forensics suite for redaction detection.

### 3.4 Source protection and secure communication

Signal for messaging; ProtonMail or Tutanota for encrypted email; SecureDrop for whistleblower intake at supported outlets; Tor Browser for source-side anonymity; air-gapped machines for the most sensitive material; physical key control for the documents.

### 3.5 Verification

EXIF data for image timestamps and location; ShadowDragon and similar OSINT suites; flight tracking (FlightRadar24); ship tracking (MarineTraffic); satellite imagery (Sentinel Hub, Planet, Google Earth historical); blockchain explorers for crypto claims.

### 3.6 Structuring and timeline-building

Timeline JS for public-facing timelines; Aeon Timeline for case work; spreadsheets (the most powerful single tool — Excel/Google Sheets for cross-reference matrices); link-analysis tools (Maltego, i2 Analyst's Notebook) for relationship mapping; mind-mapping for early-phase exploration.

### 3.7 AI-specific tools (for agents serving writers)

`web_search` and `web_fetch` for current information; `conversation_search` and `recent_chats` for context restoration across sessions; document-reading skills for PDFs, DOCX, XLSX; code execution for data work; specialised skills (`pdf-reading`, `xlsx`) for format-specific work.

---

## PART 4 — FORMAT SELECTION

The format determines what the piece can carry. Pick deliberately.

| Format               | When it fits                                                                     | Word range      | Evidence load                       |
|----------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| News brief           | One new fact, public interest, time-sensitive                                    | 200–500         | One T1 + one T3                     |
| Backgrounder         | Reader needs context to understand running story                                 | 600–1,500       | Multiple T3 + T4                    |
| Feature              | Human-anchored, narrative arc, deeper analysis                                   | 1,200–3,000     | T1 anchor + T2 corroboration + T3   |
| Investigative report | New wrongdoing, multi-source, legal review needed                                | 2,500–8,000     | T1 dominant, T2 critical, T3 framing|
| Longform / longread  | Story large enough to sustain narrative across days of reading                   | 4,000–15,000    | Layered all tiers + scenes          |
| Explainer            | Concept or process the reader needs to understand                                | 800–2,000       | T3 + T4, structured for clarity     |
| Op-ed                | Argument, public-interest position, named voice                                  | 600–1,200       | T3/T4 with named author authority   |
| Q&A                  | Subject's own voice carries the piece                                            | 800–3,000       | T1 (the interview itself)           |
| Sidebar              | Adjacent fact-set that supports a main piece without diluting it                 | 200–600         | Tight T1 or T4                      |
| Live blog            | Developing event, multiple updates, low individual claim-weight                  | Cumulative      | Source per item, low individual bar |

Pick the smallest format that carries the evidence. Inflating format weakens the piece; under-formatting buries it.

---

## PART 5 — SOURCE TIERING FOR JOURNALISM

A re-statement of §2.4 with journalism-specific examples, because tiering matters most where it is most often muddled.

- **T1.** A document you obtained and authenticated; a recording you made; an on-record interview; a public filing you read; a press conference you attended.
- **T2.** The subject's prior public statements; their social media; their press releases; their internal communications (when obtained legitimately).
- **T3.** Other reporters' work; experts you consulted; policy frameworks; scholarly literature; NGO reports.
- **T4.** Statutes; regulations; case law; datasets; dictionaries of record; encyclopaedias.

The most common professional failure is letting T3 do the work of T1 — citing other reporters' findings as if you had verified them yourself. The reader cannot tell, but legal review will, and so will subsequent reporting that disproves the chain.

---

## PART 6 — LOGICS AND REASONING PATTERNS

### 6.1 Burden scaling

The burden of proof scales with the damage the claim does. Naming someone as a witness: low burden. Naming someone as having committed wrongdoing: very high burden. A piece that mixes claim-weights without scaling its evidence is legally and ethically fragile.

### 6.2 Triangulation hierarchy

A document corroborates a witness. A witness corroborates a document. A second independent witness elevates the claim further. Forensic data (timestamps, metadata, sensor records) anchors all of the above. Anonymous sources hold lowest weight unless multiple independent ones converge on the same fact-set.

### 6.3 The cliquet / cascade logic

In any environment with parallel adjudicators (regulators, courts, professional bodies, certifiers, editorial boards), findings compound. Once one body has ruled, every subsequent decision-maker treats that ruling as evidence. Plan the *sequence* of submissions, not just their content. Sometimes the right move is to file the easiest one first to generate a finding that strengthens the harder ones.

### 6.4 Pattern induction vs canon

Some methods (admissions reading, timestamp discipline, document tiering) are externally grounded and generalise. Others (the cliquet calculus as applied to the CELTA case, the ten-element submission structure) were induced from one case. Label them as induced patterns when you teach them; reapply with care; do not treat them as canon.

### 6.5 Right of reply

Before publication, every named subject of significant claims gets the chance to respond. Send specific allegations, not vague gestures. Give a deadline that is fair (typically 48–72 hours for breaking stories, a week for non-urgent investigations). Document the request and any response, in full, in the file. If the subject does not respond, say so in the piece. This is both ethical and legally protective.

---

## PART 7 — TIME AND INVESTMENT, REALISTICALLY

Where time actually goes, by piece type:

| Piece type            | Active research  | Drafting    | Verification + legal | Total realistic |
|-----------------------|------------------|-------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| News brief            | 1–4 hours        | 1 hour      | 1 hour               | 3–6 hours       |
| Backgrounder          | 2–5 days         | 1–2 days    | 1 day                | 1 week          |
| Feature               | 1–3 weeks        | 3–5 days    | 2–4 days             | 3–5 weeks       |
| Investigation         | 1–6 months       | 2–4 weeks   | 2–6 weeks            | 3–12 months     |
| Longform book chapter | 2–6 months       | 1–2 months  | 1 month              | 6–9 months      |

Where the time goes that surprises new investigators: document acquisition (waiting for FOI responses, chasing down originals), source cultivation (most sources need three to five contacts before useful conversation), verification cycles (each new fact opens new verification needs), legal review (a single contested paragraph can cost a week), and editor-driven restructuring (the second half of any major piece is rewriting, not writing).

The CELTA case is in the *investigation* band. Approximately five months from disclosure (25 January) to a structurally complete dossier (mid-May). That timeline is *normal* for this kind of work, not slow.

---

## PART 8 — HICCUPS: A TYPOLOGY AND RESPONSES

Things that go wrong, with their corrective patterns.

**H1 — A source goes silent.** Most common cause: pressure from their employer or counsel. Do not chase. Document the timing. Note that silence is itself evidence if it follows a specific question. Cultivate a parallel source on the same fact.

**H2 — A document is denied or heavily redacted.** Appeal the redaction (CADA in France, ICO in UK, FOIA appeal in US). Document the denial. Sometimes the denial itself is the story (over-redaction signals importance).

**H3 — Legal threat / cease-and-desist.** Read carefully. Most are bluffs. A real legal threat will be specific about which claim, which jurisdiction, which cause of action. Respond formally, factually, and only after legal review. Do not pull the piece on the threat alone; pull only if the underlying claim is factually wrong.

**H4 — The subject mounts a PR counter-campaign.** Stay on document. Do not engage with the PR. Continue publishing on the original evidence base. Counter-PR strengthens *your* position by making the subject's anxiety visible.

**H5 — Discovery of error after publication.** Correct quickly, prominently, and specifically. The audience trusts corrections more than denials. Note the error, the correction, and the date. Do not bury it.

**H6 — Bounced contact / wasted lead.** (The DREETS bounce, the QUALITIA candidate-list.) Verify at the point of use. When a public entity is legally required to display information, search for the actual display before listing candidates.

**H7 — Memory loss across sessions or staff turnover.** Externalise method into portable files. The agent's SKILL.md is the journalist's case-file methodology document. Both exist because human and AI memory both reset.

**H8 — Editor restructures the piece.** Expect it. The piece you deliver and the piece that publishes will differ. Hold the line on factual claims; concede on structure, ordering, and tone within reason.

**H9 — Source identity is exposed.** Catastrophic if avoidable. Plan source protection from first contact. Never email a sensitive source from a work account. Use single-use channels. Compartmentalise.

**H10 — You catch yourself inventing a specific to keep momentum.** Stop. Verify or flag. The "eagerness anti-pattern" — known from the AI context but identical in human writers under deadline pressure — is the single most common cause of corrections.

---

## PART 9 — THE METHODS THE AI USED IN THESE TWO CHATS

For agents reading this guideline, a transparent log of what worked in the source sessions.

The agent ran `recent_chats` and `conversation_search` repeatedly to rebuild context — never assumed continuity. It read both chats before responding to a synthesis request, not just the most recent one. It performed live `web_search` before stating any authority address, fee, or article number, and produced a verified contact table only after multiple search confirmations. When the user provided a Claude.ai share link, the agent acknowledged it could not fetch the link and asked for context restoration in another form, rather than confabulating prior content. The agent externalised its working method into SKILL.md files mid-case, before the session ended, so the method would survive. The agent maintained administrative-neutral register across all drafted correspondence, and refused to characterise the opposing party's conduct in writing even when the user implicitly invited it. The agent named the inductive nature of certain patterns (the cliquet calculus) rather than presenting them as established canon.

The agent's main self-correction during the case: an authoritative-sounding DREETS email turned out to be wrong; the lesson was codified into the Phase 9 verification discipline in the SKILL files.

---

## PART 10 — DECISION DICE FOR THE WRITER

A compact lookup, oriented to investigative work.

| Situation                                                              | Reach for                                              |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| New tip, unclear whether story                                         | T1 acquisition first; do not draft yet                 |
| Adversarial correspondence in hand                                     | Read for admissions, build cross-reference matrix      |
| Multiple regulators / parallel adjudicators                            | Cliquet logic; plan sequence of filings or queries     |
| Claim that ruins reputation                                            | Triple-source; legal review; right of reply           |
| Subject says "we take this seriously"                                  | Search for the *specific actions* they describe       |
| Document with a blank field where content was expected                 | Absence-as-evidence; cite the template structure       |
| Story arc not yet visible                                              | Build timeline before drafting                         |
| Tempted to cite another reporter's finding as if your own              | Don't; verify or attribute clearly                     |
| About to publish without giving subject a chance to respond            | Stop; send specific allegations with deadline          |
| Subject responds "please simplify, I don't understand"                 | 3–5 items, tied to evidence, with deadline             |
| Tempted to add one more lead before publication                        | If admission already in writing, stop and publish      |
| Method has stabilised across the case                                  | Write it down as a portable file                       |
| Verifiable specific about to enter your draft (address, fee, article)  | Search at the moment of use                            |
| Editor's restructure changes a factual claim                           | Push back; concede on structure, not facts             |
| You feel yourself reaching to invent a specific                        | Stop; verify or flag uncertainty                       |

---

## PART 11 — CASE STUDY (THE ANCHORING EXAMPLE)

For pattern-recognition purposes, the CELTA dossier in compressed form.

**Trigger:** A candidate on a Cambridge CELTA course in Brittany disclosed a recognised disability (ADHD + depression, French ALD status) by email on 25 January 2026 at 16:00. The course tutor acknowledged at 16:12.

**What the documents subsequently showed:** A pre-decision meeting (assessor + tutors) on 30 January, four days before the final teaching practice (TP8), without the candidate (WhatsApp 31 January 13:55 documents this). A Stage-Three tutorial signed two days after the disclosure with no reference to the disclosure. An assessor report covering TP4 and TP7 but not TP8. A summative record amended without annotation. The centre's named disability contact, Jane Ryder, never contacted the candidate after the disclosure although she was the named disability contact in the signed Candidate Agreement. Jane Ryder's 1 March email listed four categories of accommodation that existed (document format, audio, extra tutor time, deadline extensions), conceding by implication that none had been offered.

**Third-party confirmation:** Cambridge's own Stage One appellate report (23 April 2026) confirmed the unannotated amendment and described "only two components of assessment" against a syllabus that names six holistic factors.

**The decisive five documents:** the disclosure email + 16:12 acknowledgement; the Ryder 1 March admission; the signed Candidate Agreement; the WhatsApp message from 31 January; the Stage One report. Plus the medical synthesis from Dr Pauvert (11 February).

**The investigative pattern this case fits:** institutional defendant; protected-characteristic disclosure unaccommodated; assessment defects; internal appeal that confirmed rather than examined; multiple regulators with overlapping jurisdiction; documents that admit elements when read for admissions rather than arguments. This pattern recurs in education, healthcare, employment, financial services, prisons, immigration adjudication, and disability access cases. Any reporter or writer working in those areas can apply the same method.

**The central lesson:** the case was not won by acquiring more evidence. It was won by *moving the existing admissions, with documentary discipline, through parallel threshold-meeting submissions*. The documents did the work. The writer's job was organisation, not amplification.

---

*End of guideline. Carry it across cases. Adapt it deliberately. Honour the documentary discipline above all.*
-e 
---
# FILE: GUIDELINE_methods_and_case_study.md
## Source: /mnt/project/GUIDELINE_methods_and_case_study.md
---

# Methods, Logic, and Sources — A Guideline With Case Study for Future LLM Agents

**Derived from:** two working sessions on the Sourov Deb / CELTA / The ELT Hub dossier (15 May 2026)
**Purpose:** capture the operational pattern that worked, so future agents can apply, adapt, or recognise it
**Register:** essay, concise paragraphs, no inflation

---

## PART 1 — WHAT THE TWO CHATS WERE

Both chats handled the same underlying case from different angles. The first chat (`fdd392d5…`) opened with a context-restoration problem (a Claude.ai share link could not be fetched), then rebuilt the dossier from the documents on disk, drafted the Jane Ryder GDPR communication, and ended by producing two consolidated SKILL.md files for future agents. The second chat (`4c2e2357…`) ran a cross-jurisdictional legality analysis (UK/French law against the Cambridge Stage One report) and then produced a verified authority cartography — eighteen authorities across six tiers, contacts checked by live web search.

The case itself: a candidate disclosed a disability mid-CELTA, was failed despite contemporaneous tutor statements indicating a Pass was warranted, and the centre's later correspondence inadvertently admitted that accommodations existed and were not offered. The work across both chats was to turn that admission, plus a small set of timestamped documents, into a structurally complete dossier for multiple regulators in parallel.

---

## PART 2 — METHODS USED, IN SMALL PARAGRAPHS

### 2.1 Documentary forensics over argument

The single most load-bearing method was reading documents for what they *concede incidentally*, not for what they argue. Jane Ryder's 1 March email was central not because of its defensive framing but because it listed four accommodation categories to *explain* the centre's position — and that list also enumerated, by necessity, what the centre had not done. The logic: in adversarial correspondence, defenders explain themselves; explanations admit elements. Source: legal-evidentiary tradition, internalised into the dossier as Phase 2 of the SKILL.

### 2.2 Timestamp discipline

Every duty triggered by knowledge runs on a clock. The disclosure email at 16:00 and Simon Brooks's acknowledgement at 16:12 are not "the same afternoon" — they are "twelve minutes." A timestamp cited to the minute is structurally stronger than the same fact cited to the day. This came from the EA 2010 s.20 / Code du travail L.5213-6 framework, where the anticipatory duty engages at the moment of knowledge.

### 2.3 The cross-reference matrix (party says / document shows / law requires)

For every contested point, three columns: what the opposing party asserts, what the document actually shows, what the applicable law requires. Divergences become the case. This is the diagnostic that produces the asymmetric-threshold pattern (e.g., the centre demanded perfect contemporaneous documentation from the candidate while accepting its own undocumented record amendment). Source: standard legal-analysis practice, adapted for tabular use.

### 2.4 The cliquet (ratchet) effect across parallel authorities

Eighteen authorities were mapped, twelve with binding power, six consultative. The logic is not "file everywhere and hope." It is conditional probability: once one authority issues a finding against the centre, that finding becomes evidence in every subsequent proceeding, raising the conditional probability of further findings. Two binding findings out of twelve is a credible target; three to five over six months follows structurally. Source: emerged from the case — labelled with the French legal term *cliquet* (ratchet) which describes irreversible rights-acquisition in social law.

### 2.5 Document tiering (T1 primary, T2 adversarial, T3 contextual, T4 reference)

Outputs lead with T1 (the dossier's own primary evidence — disclosure email, signed agreement, medical synthesis), anchor in T2 (the opponent's own correspondence — most valuable for admissions), support with T3 (policies, frameworks, syllabi), and cite T4 (codes, articles, regulations). Mixing tiers without hierarchy is the most common drafting failure. Source: research-methods tradition, repurposed for legal dossiers.

### 2.6 Verified web search before stating any contact

In Chat 2, every authority email and postal address was checked by live `web_search` before being placed in the table. This was a direct response to a failure earlier in the case: an authoritative-looking address (`dreets-bretagne@dreets.gouv.fr`) bounced; verification produced the correct one (`bretag.controle-fp@dreets.gouv.fr`). Source: an empirical lesson from a real bounce, now codified as Phase 9 of the SKILL.

### 2.7 Forensic autoethnography as the academic frame

When the same material was reformatted for academic register, the methodology was named: forensic autoethnography (Denzin 2014; Ellis 2004; Sparkes 2000). The participant-observer's insider access combined with the forensic standard of chain-of-custody and contradiction mapping. The three-lane analytical structure (procedural-legal / assessment-validity / documentary-integrity) keeps evidentiary standards distinct across claim types. Source: methodological literature in qualitative inquiry.

### 2.8 Administrative neutrality in drafting tone

Every external communication — to Jane, to CNIL, to DREETS, to Ofqual — held the same tonal register: administrative formality, documentary precision, legal specificity by article number, concession of legitimate scope, facts stated rather than threats made. When the user asked "did we mention her silence and comportment?" — the answer was no, deliberately. Characterising the opponent's conduct dilutes administrative neutrality and weakens the document. Source: the practical observation that experienced investigators read tone first and substance second.

### 2.9 The ten-element submission structure

Every regulatory submission carries: complainant block, respondent block, subject-matter block, status block, chronological narrative, decisive evidence (3–5 facts cited), legal basis (articles by number), specific demand, document repository link, signature block. Missing any one weakens admissibility. Source: pattern-induced from comparing successful French and UK regulatory submissions during the case.

### 2.10 Length discipline

Authorities do not read long submissions. Email body 300–500 words; cover-letter PDF 1–3 pages; appendix 5–10 pages; character-limited forms 95 % of cap. The decisive facts survive every cut. The narrative is what gets pruned. Source: empirical — investigators have caseloads, and length is a filtering signal for them.

### 2.11 Skill-file generation as durable memory

At the end of both chats, the working method was extracted into SKILL.md files (`disability-training-appeals`, `complex-document-corpus-analysis`, `multi-document-regulatory-case-analysis`). These survive the session boundary that LLM agents otherwise reset across. The logic: agents cannot carry context across sessions reliably, but the user can carry a file. The skill file is portable memory. Source: the Anthropic skills framework itself, applied to user-side continuity.

### 2.12 Restraint when the case is structurally complete

When the centre admitted the case's elements in writing, the user was told to *stop sending material* and let the documents work. The instinct on both sides — agent's to keep adding, user's to keep clarifying — was identified as a dilution risk. The "I don't understand, please simplify" reply from the opponent was named for what it usually is: a stalling tactic, not sincere confusion. Source: case-management practice in regulatory work.

---

## PART 3 — WHERE EACH METHOD CAME FROM (SOURCING SUMMARY)

A short map of provenance, because it matters for whether a method generalises:

The legal frameworks (EA 2010, RGPD, Code du travail, Qualiopi, Ofqual G8/I4) are external and stable — found in statute and verified by web search at the moment of citation. The forensic-reading methods (admissions, timestamps, signatures, missing entries) are adapted from legal-evidentiary practice and qualitative-research forensics. The cliquet/ratchet logic and the ten-element submission structure emerged inductively from the case — they are pattern observations, not pre-existing canons, though *cliquet* is borrowed terminology from French social law. The autoethnographic framing is academic methodology, properly cited. The verification protocol and the SKILL.md externalisation came from agent self-correction during the case (a bounced email; a wasted candidate list).

The point: the externally-sourced methods generalise immediately. The inductively-derived ones generalise to *structurally similar cases* — multi-document, multi-authority, extended-timeline, disclosure-of-protected-characteristic cases — but should be reapplied with care to other shapes.

---

## PART 4 — ADVICE FOR FUTURE EVENTS, BY ROLE

### 4.1 If you are a researcher

Use forensic autoethnography (or its non-autoethnographic equivalent: forensic document analysis with named participant) when your case is *anchored in a documentary corpus you possess in full*. Build the document tier (T1–T4) before writing a word of analysis. Run the three-lane separation early; it prevents claim-type contamination later. Cite to the minute when timestamps exist. State your positionality, and let methodological transparency carry the credibility that strict objectivity cannot. Where you derive a pattern inductively from one case (as with the ratchet effect here), label it as such and propose it as a hypothesis for replication, not a law.

### 4.2 If you are an AI/LLM agent

Read the chat history fully before responding when the user signals they're returning to prior work — the linguistic cues are possessives without antecedent ("my dossier," "our case") and definite articles assuming shared reference ("the centre," "that email"). Search before stating any address, contact, fee, article number, or named role-holder — training data is too stale for these and silent errors are costly. Build the cross-reference matrix before drafting any substantive output. Hold tonal register: administrative, factual, article-cited. Externalise method into a SKILL.md whenever a working pattern stabilises — the user can carry it across sessions even when you cannot. When a case is structurally complete, say so and stop adding. Recognise the eagerness anti-pattern in yourself (inventing details to keep momentum) and the omniscience anti-pattern (confident statements about verifiable facts). Both fail the user.

### 4.3 If you are a content creator

Lead with the admission, not the grievance. A reader who arrives at "the centre admitted in writing that four accommodations existed and none were offered" engages differently than one who arrives at "I was treated unfairly." Hold length discipline — the long version dilutes; the tight version compels. Use tier-1 documents at decisive points only, not throughout; over-citation reads as defensive. Keep the administrative register for primary pieces and let the emotional register live only in clearly-marked personal sections, if at all. Where you are tempted to characterise an antagonist's conduct, describe their *documents* instead — it is more devastating because the reader does the work.

### 4.4 Cross-role rules

Three rules apply to all three roles. First: never invent specifics — names, citations, case numbers, dates, precedents. The cost of one fabricated fact in a regulatory or scholarly context is the credibility of the entire dossier. Second: verify at the point of statement, not at the point of training. Authorities, addresses, articles, fees, and personnel change; treat your prior knowledge as a hypothesis to check. Third: respect the user's pacing. Sustained engagement over months beats spasms of effort; the agent's job includes recognising when to pause.

---

## PART 5 — DECISION DICE: WHICH METHOD FOR WHICH SITUATION

A compact lookup. The left column is the situation you face; the right is the method to reach for first.

| Situation                                                                 | Reach for                                       |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Adversarial correspondence in front of you                                | Read for admissions, not arguments (§2.1)       |
| Duty-of-knowledge clock running                                           | Timestamp discipline (§2.2)                     |
| Contested factual point with three plausible accounts                     | Cross-reference matrix (§2.3)                   |
| Multiple authorities with overlapping jurisdiction                        | Cliquet / ratchet calculus (§2.4)               |
| Document pile with no obvious hierarchy                                   | Tier (T1–T4) before analysing (§2.5)            |
| About to state an email, address, fee, article number                     | Verify by live search first (§2.6)              |
| Academic write-up of a personal case                                      | Forensic autoethnography (§2.7)                 |
| Drafting any external communication                                       | Administrative neutrality (§2.8)                |
| Regulatory submission                                                     | Ten-element structure (§2.9)                    |
| Tempted to send a long submission                                         | Length discipline (§2.10)                       |
| Working method has stabilised across a case                               | Externalise to SKILL.md (§2.11)                 |
| Case admits its own elements                                              | Stop adding; let documents work (§2.12)         |
| Opponent says "I don't understand, simplify"                              | Re-state demand in 3–5 items, end exchange      |
| Returning user references "our case" / "the dossier"                      | Read chat history before answering              |
| You catch yourself inventing a specific to keep momentum                  | Stop, verify, or flag uncertainty               |

---

## PART 6 — CASE STUDY (THE ANCHORING EXAMPLE)

A future agent reading this guideline will benefit from one fully-worked example. Here it is in compressed form.

**Trigger:** a candidate disclosed disability (TDAH + depression, ALD-recognised) on 25 January 2026 at 16:00, acknowledged 16:12 by tutor.

**Structural defects later documented:** the centre held a TP-framework decision meeting on 30 January, four days before TP8, without the candidate (WhatsApp 31 Jan 13:55); the Stage-Three tutorial signed two days post-disclosure contained no reference to the disclosure; an assessor report covered TP4 and TP7 but not TP8; the summative record was amended without annotation; the centre's named disability contact (Jane Ryder) was never contacted by the centre after the disclosure though she was named in the signed Candidate Agreement; on 1 March Jane Ryder wrote that four categories of accommodation existed (document format, audio, extra tutor time, deadline extensions) — none had been offered.

**Third-party confirmation:** Cambridge Stage One report (23 April 2026) confirmed the unannotated amendment and described "only two components of assessment" against a syllabus that names six holistic factors.

**Documentary core (the decisive five):** disclosure email + 16:12 acknowledgement; Ryder 1 March admission; signed Candidate Agreement; WhatsApp 31 January; Stage One report. Plus medical synthesis (Dr Pauvert, 11 February).

**Authorities engaged in parallel:** QUALITIA Certification (the actual Qualiopi certificateur), France Compétences, CNIL, DREETS Bretagne, Défenseur des droits (reopening with comparator), MDPH (RQTH), Ofqual, EHRC, ICO; with five more in supporting tiers.

**Outcome calculus on neutral logic:** structurally available findings on at least nine of the twelve binding authorities; ratchet effect makes subsequent findings more probable than the first; the case does not depend on any single authority's disposition.

**The lesson:** the case did not need additional evidence. It needed *discipline in moving the existing admissions* through parallel, threshold-meeting submissions. The documents did the work. The agent organised. The user decided.

---

*End of guideline. Apply to multi-document, multi-authority, extended-timeline cases involving disclosed-protected-characteristic disputes. Adapt with care to other shapes. Honour the documentary discipline above all.*
-e 
---

# SOUROV DEB — SYSTEM HANDOVER
**Site:** sourovdeb.com | **Owner:** Sourov DEB | sourovdeb.is@gmail.com | sourovdeb@zohomail.com
**Date:** 2026-05-25 | **Status:** ✅ Production Ready

---

## 1. FILE MANAGER ACCESS (How to Deploy Anything)

### Primary Gateway — deploy.php (NO FTP client needed)
```
URL:    https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php
Secret: 0767044896thevenet_
```

**Commands:**
```bash
# Upload file
CONTENT=$(base64 -w 0 < file.php)
curl -X POST "https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php?key=0767044896thevenet_" \
  --data-urlencode "action=upload" \
  --data-urlencode "path=wp-content/plugins/filename.php" \
  --data-urlencode "encoded=true" \
  --data-urlencode "content=$CONTENT"

# List directory
curl "https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php?action=list&key=0767044896thevenet_&path=wp-content/plugins/"

# Download file
curl "https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php?action=download&key=0767044896thevenet_&path=wp-content/plugins/filename.php" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json,base64; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(base64.b64decode(d['content']).decode())"

# Delete file
curl -X POST "https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php?key=0767044896thevenet_" \
  --data-urlencode "action=delete" \
  --data-urlencode "path=public_html/filename.php"

# Status check
curl "https://www.sourovdeb.com/deploy.php?action=status&key=0767044896thevenet_"
```

### Path Mapping (Critical)
```
deploy path=wp-content/X    → /public_html/wp-content/X    (plugins, themes)
deploy path=public_html/X   → /public_html/public_html/X   (WordPress root files)
deploy path=wp-config.php   → /public_html/wp-config.php   (server root)
```

### FTP (Backup — use deploy.php instead)
```
Host:     ftp.sourovdeb.com
User:     u839078121.sourov
Password: 0767044896Thevenet_
Port:     21
```

### Hostinger Server

WordPress:     /home/u839078121/domains/sourovdeb.com/public_html/public_html/

```

---

## 2. WORKFLOW — How It All Works

```
CONTENT CREATION (You)
  ↓
Google Sheet: "WordPress Publishing Queue"
Sheet ID: 1fJX0yZNe0tjrQ1YZU0iP0kLWU0r3qwx6-J2ODAUGRIE
Tab: Sheet1
  ↓
Set column "Approved" = TRUE
  ↓
Apps Script (WP_PUBLISHING_QUEUE.gs) triggers
  ↓
REST API call → sourov-ai-controller.php
Endpoint: POST /wp-json/sourov/v1/ai-post
Auth: X-Sourov-Key: 0767044896thevenet_
  ↓
Post created/scheduled in WordPress
  ↓
aicu-engine-reach.php auto-pings IndexNow
(Notifies Bing, Yandex, Naver instantly)
  ↓
Yoast SEO updates sitemap
  ↓
LiteSpeed Cache clears once
  ↓
Post live + indexed within 24-48h
```

### Google Sheet Columns (Sheet1)
```
A: Title          → WordPress post title
B: Content        → Post body (HTML ok)
C: Category       → Category name
D: Tags           → Comma-separated tags
E: Meta Description → Yoast meta description
F: SEO Title      → Yoast SEO title
G: Publish Date   → ISO date (future = scheduled) or blank (immediate)
H: Status         → Written back: Published / Scheduled / ERROR
I: Approved       → TRUE triggers publish ← YOUR TRIGGER
J: Post ID        → Written back by script
K: Result Log     → Timestamp + status written back
```

### Apps Script
```
File: WP_PUBLISHING_QUEUE.gs
Project: https://script.google.com/home/projects/1NCi2zMmZqUtGsEORwx2kjh8CS8inwdcjwnhUagDESfNqIv6T6rDsyK1N/edit
SHEET_NAME: Sheet1
WP_ENDPOINT: https://www.sourovdeb.com/wp-json/sourov/v1/ai-post
WP_API_KEY:  0767044896thevenet_
```

---

## 3. PLUGIN ROLES (What Each Does)

### sourov-ai-controller.php (v1.1) — PUBLISHER
```
Role:    Creates/schedules WordPress posts via REST API
Auth:    X-Sourov-Key header
Endpoints:
  POST   /wp-json/sourov/v1/ai-post     ← Main publishing endpoint
  GET    /wp-json/sourov/v1/scheduled   ← List scheduled posts
  POST   /wp-json/sourov/v1/bulk        ← Bulk publish array
  DELETE /wp-json/sourov/v1/post/{id}   ← Delete post
  GET    /wp-json/sourov/v1/status      ← Health check (public)
Hook priority: 20 (conflict-safe)
```

### aicu-engine-reach.php (v1.0.0) — INDEXER
```
Role:    Auto-notifies search engines on every publish/update/delete
Method:  IndexNow protocol (Bing, Yandex, Naver, Seznam, Yep)
IndexNow key: 61844e81205e89682bc4562b4b690e3a
Key URL: https://sourovdeb.com/61844e81205e89682bc4562b4b690e3a.txt
Also:    Injects JSON-LD schema, manages robots.txt, serves llms.txt
Note:    Google uses Search Console separately (already verified)
```

### sourov-automation-agent.php — VERIFIER
```
Role:    Manages search engine verification meta tags
Admin:   WordPress → Automation → Bing & Other
Status:  Bing tag injected ✅ | Google verified via DNS ✅
Endpoints: /wp-json/sourov-automation/v1/*
```

### sourov-diagnostic-agent.php — MONITOR
```
Role:    Read-only system monitoring (never modifies anything)
Endpoints (all public):
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/health     ← Quick health check
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/wordpress  ← WP version, posts, users
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/plugins    ← All plugins + status
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/cache      ← Cache status
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/errors     ← Last 50 error log lines
  /wp-json/sourov-diagnostic/v1/report     ← Full report (all above)
```

### aicu-engine-reach.php ALSO handles:
- robots.txt AI bot rules (appended to Yoast's block)
- JSON-LD Article/Author/Organization schema
- llms.txt (list of recent posts for AI models)

### Third-party plugins (do not touch):
```
litespeed-cache        → Page caching (LiteSpeed server)
wordpress-seo (Yoast)  → XML sitemaps, meta tags
google-site-kit        → Google Analytics integration
google-analytics       → MonsterInsights Analytics
astra-sites            → Theme starter templates
```

---

## 4. SEARCH ENGINE STATUS

```
Google Search Console: ✅ Verified (DNS TXT record)
  Property: https://sourovdeb.com
  Verification: TXT @ → google-site-verification=8KvpLQQ5kQqr418svxq6YyW...
  Sitemaps: Submitted (sitemap_index.xml + sitemap.xml)
  Indexing: 1+ pages, growing

Bing Webmaster: ✅ Verified (meta tag)
  Meta tag: BF2B5489CAEF5D3D7598D5FD07DF0755
  Injected via: sourov-automation-agent.php
  Sitemaps: Submitted
